summary:
Oracle bone divination was the central ritual institution of the late [[event:55|Shang Dynasty]], practiced at the royal court from approximately 1300 to 1046 BC. Divination specialists applied intense heat to the shoulder bones of cattle (scapulimancy) or to turtle plastrons, then interpreted the resulting cracks as messages from royal ancestors and high deities. Scribes subsequently carved the divination questions, the crack readings, and sometimes the verified outcomes directly into the bone or shell surface, creating an archive of extraordinary historical detail.

The inscriptions preserved on oracle bones constitute the oldest substantial body of Chinese writing and the earliest textual source for Chinese history. Topics addressed in the divinations include the military campaigns of specific kings, the health and reproductive prospects of the royal family, weather and harvest conditions, the scheduling of hunts and sacrifices, and inquiries about the cause of royal ailments or misfortunes. The [[event:1841|Yin Xu capital at Anyang]] has yielded over 150,000 oracle bone fragments, and scholarly study of the inscriptions has expanded dramatically since the early twentieth century.

The oracle bone script contains several thousand distinct characters, of which approximately a third can be linked to later Chinese writing forms. The system was logographic, with individual signs representing words or morphemes rather than sounds alone, though phonetic components were incorporated in many characters. After the Zhou conquest in 1046 BC, the practice of oracle bone divination declined sharply, superseded by bronze vessel inscriptions and other media. However, the oracle bone corpus remains invaluable for understanding Shang religious beliefs, social structure, and political geography, as well as for tracing the evolution of the Chinese writing system.
